questions.question: Which language CANNOT be described by a regular grammar but CAN be described by a context-free grammar?
options: { aⁿbⁿ | n ≥ 0 }
{ w | w contains an even number of a's }
{ w | w ends with 'ab' }
questions.explanation: { aⁿbⁿ } requires counting/matching which exceeds finite memory. The pumping lemma proves it is not regular, but the grammar S → aSb | ε shows it is context-free.

questions.question: In a CFG defined as G = (V, Σ, R, S), what does the production rule notation A → α mean?
options: α must be a single terminal symbol
A is replaced by α in one derivation step, regardless of surrounding context
α can only contain variables from V
A and α must both be terminal symbols
questions.explanation: A → α means A is replaced by α in one step. α can be any string of terminals and variables (or ε). The grammar is 'context-free' because the replacement of A does not depend on what symbols surround it.

questions.question: A grammar is ambiguous when:
options: It has more variables than terminals
Some terminal string has two or more distinct parse trees
It contains epsilon productions
It cannot generate all strings in its language
questions.explanation: Ambiguity means there exist multiple parse trees (or equivalently, multiple leftmost derivations) for the same terminal string. This matters for programming languages because different parse trees imply different meanings.

questions.question: Why is the grammar S → E, E → E + T | T, T → T * F | F, F → (E) | id ambiguous?
options: It uses both variables and terminals
The string 'id + id * id' has two parse trees depending on operator precedence
It contains unit productions
It cannot derive the string 'id'
questions.explanation: The string 'id + id * id' has two parse trees: one where + groups first ((id+id)*id) and one where * groups first (id+(id*id)). The grammar does not encode precedence, so both structures are valid parse trees.

questions.question: What makes a grammar 'context-free'?
options: It can only generate finite languages
Every production rule has a single nonterminal on the left-hand side
It has no epsilon productions
It can only produce strings of even length
questions.explanation: In a context-free grammar (CFG), each production rule has exactly one nonterminal on the left side (e.g., A → α). This means the replacement of A doesn't depend on surrounding context. Context-sensitive grammars allow multiple symbols on the left side.
